policy_statement: Apply renewable energy mandates for new buildings (NREAP policy instrument).
mechanism_description: The policy mandates renewable energy in new buildings, leading to more renewable energy use that displaces fossil-based generation and lowers emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy sourcing set → ↑ renewable generation/use → ↓ fossil fuel combustion in grid/heat supply → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building design and operations are set to include a minimum share/amount of renewable energy (typically via onsite generation like solar PV/solar thermal, or verified offsite renewable procurement) for new construction.  
2) Mediating process: Additional renewable energy generation supplies part of the building’s energy demand, thereby reducing net demand served by fossil-based generators (and/or reducing fossil boiler fuel use if renewable thermal is used).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂ (and related) emissions from displaced fossil electricity/heat production over the building’s operational life.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that mandated renewables for new buildings displace fossil-based electricity/heat generation, reducing GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: A mandated shift to renewable energy in buildings increases renewable supply and displaces fossil generation, directly cutting operational GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can also improve energy security or reduce operating costs (resource efficiency co-benefits), the mechanism’s core intent and largest climate effect is emissions reduction via fuel-switching/supply decarbonization.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the requirement is met with low-additionality instruments (e.g., purchasing unbundled RECs with minimal real-world displacement) the realized mitigation may be smaller, but the canonical mechanism remains fossil displacement/mitigation. If renewables are paired with electrification increasing load, net impacts depend on grid mix; the requirement still targets lowering net fossil generation share.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that mandates renewable energy in new buildings operates through increasing renewable energy use and reducing fossil-supplied energy, which is a mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further subtype (e.g., onsite PV vs offsite procurement; electricity vs thermal; stringency/coverage) must be assigned using policy-specific evidence, not the shared mechanism alone.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include compliance via low-additionality renewable certificates, rebound/increased electricity demand from added end-uses, or renewable systems with significant embodied emissions; these affect magnitude but not the primary mitigation classification for this mechanism.
key_indicators: “Mandates”; “renewable energy mandates for new buildings”; “established to realize the targets”
instance_edge_case_notes: Could alternatively be implemented via a building-code performance standard (e.g., minimum renewable share), but the source text explicitly frames it as a mandate, so classified as regulatory-mandate.
